ground_truth.title: Rust Networking for xv6
ground_truth.author: Alessandro Ferrari
ground_truth.main_content: In my previous post I explained how to compile and link Rust modules for the x86 target, showing how to print a simple message from rust to the xv6 console. In this guide I will expand on the framework explained above, implementing interrupt-driven networking with a single TCP socket (similar to *BSD) in the xv6 kernel, fully written in Rust, using the Intel e1000 network card. On top of this, this project is built on the public xv6 source meaning that I can show the full repo. I will then show this functionality by telling ChatGPT 5.1 what syscalls it has access to and making it write a userspace HTTP server. There are a lot of great guides explaining how the e1000 works and how to interface to it in xv6. On a very basic level: You first initialize the card by sending it a series of numbers over PCI. When the e1000 NIC receives a packet, it writes it to a circular buffer and generates an interrupt. When you want to transmit a packet, you write a packet to a separate circular buffer and set a flag. The kernel should therefore map these buffers into memory and then use MMIO to read and write to the network card. How I chose to implement this was by having four main functions: e1000_init(void) which reads card, maps memory, sends codes to set the card in the right mode; e1000_tx(data, len) which given ethernet frames writes packets to the circular buffer; e1000_rx_poll(buf, len) to check the NIC RX ring and read packets; and e1000_intr(void) as the interrupt handler. I started the project by implementing my own TCP/IP stack, however I realized early on that it would have involved writing hundreds of lines of code handling every specific IP protocols that I wanted. After some research I came across smoltcp, a standalone, event-driven TCP/IP stack designed for bare-metal, real-time systems. Very importantly, this crate compiles with no_std, making it an excellent choice for xv6 development. With the smoltcp Device implementation done, we have a working TCP/IP stack that we can use from the xv6 kernel, however the user still has no way to access these resources. To give the user access to networking resources, I chose to implement 4 syscalls: net_listen, net_accept, net_recv, net_send, and net_close. On the C side, these functions simply do argument parsing and pass the control to the Rust module. The real logic is all implement in the safe Rust module. However, due to smoltcp, these syscall handlers are very light. From my linux host, I've then created a tap device and put it on the 10.0.3.1/24 network. On the xv6 device I've then hardcoded an ip and MAC address (I don't have a dhcp server running on my host). And asked ChatGPT to write a simple HTTP server using the syscalls written above. This server returns a simple message when a GET request is sent, and shuts down when someone POSTs "close". The code is accessible at github.com/Ferryistaken/xv6-public, and a full patch is pasted below.
with: In this guide I will expand on the framework explained above, implementing interrupt-driven networking with a single TCP socket (similar to *BSD) in the xv6 kernel, fully written in Rust, using the Intel e1000 network card.
On a very basic level: You first initialize the card by sending it a series of numbers over PCI.
I started the project by implementing my own TCP/IP stack, however I realized early on that it would have involved writing hundreds of lines of code handling every specific IP protocols.
After some research I came across smoltcp, a standalone, event-driven TCP/IP stack that is designed for bare-metal, real-time systems.
To give the user access to networking resources, I chose to implement 4 syscalls: net_listen, net_accept, net_recv, net_send, and net_close.

items.caption: On the left: qemu running xv6 with rust networking, on the right: linux host connecting to the server. The final `ls` output shows that sending a `close` in the post payload shuts down the server.
